In 1989, [Soviet forces withdrew from Afghanistan](/wiki/Soviet_withdrawal_from_Afghanistan "Soviet withdrawal from Afghanistan") without achieving their objectives.[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis2005235–236-344) Later that year, the [Berlin Wall](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Berlin_Wall "Fall of the Berlin Wall"), the [Inner German border](/wiki/Inner_German_border#Fall_of_the_inner_German_border "Inner German border") and the [Iron Curtain](/wiki/Iron_Curtain#Fall_of_the_Iron_Curtain "Iron Curtain") fell. On 3 December 1989, Gorbachev and Bush declared the Cold War over at the [Malta Summit](/wiki/Malta_Summit "Malta Summit"). In February 1990, Gorbachev agreed with the US-proposed [Treaty on the Final Settlement with Respect to Germany](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Final_Settlement_with_Respect_to_Germany "Treaty on the Final Settlement with Respect to Germany") and signed it on 12 September 1990, paving the way for the [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification").[[327]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShearman199576-342) When the Berlin Wall came down, Gorbachev's "[Common European Home](/wiki/Common_European_Home "Common European Home")" concept began to take shape.[[330]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEuropean_Navigator1989-345)[[331]](#cite_note-346) The two former adversaries were partners in the [Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War") against [Iraq](/wiki/Ba%27athist_Iraq "Ba'athist Iraq") (August 1990 – February 1991).[[332]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMotin2025111–112-347)[[333]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENewman199341-348) During the final summit in Moscow in July 1991, Gorbachev and Bush signed the [START I](/wiki/START_I "START I") arms control treaty.[[334]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis2005255-349)

### Eastern Europe breaks away

Main article: [Revolutions of 1989](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Oliver_Mark_-_Otto_Habsburg-Lothringen%2C_P%C3%B6cking_2006.jpg/250px-Oliver_Mark_-_Otto_Habsburg-Lothringen%2C_P%C3%B6cking_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oliver_Mark_-_Otto_Habsburg-Lothringen,_P%C3%B6cking_2006.jpg)

[Otto von Habsburg](/wiki/Otto_von_Habsburg "Otto von Habsburg"), who played a leading role in opening the [Iron Curtain](/wiki/Iron_Curtain "Iron Curtain")

Two developments dominated the decade that followed: the increasingly apparent crumbling of the Soviet Union's economic and political structures, and the patchwork attempts at reforms to reverse that process. Kenneth S. Deffeyes argued in *[Beyond Oil](/wiki/Beyond_Oil "Beyond Oil")* that the [Reagan administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_Ronald_Reagan "Presidency of Ronald Reagan") encouraged [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") to [lower the price of oil](/wiki/1980s_oil_glut "1980s oil glut") to the point where the Soviets could not make a profit selling their oil, and resulted in the depletion of the country's [hard currency](/wiki/Hard_currency "Hard currency") reserves.[[335]](#cite_note-350)

Brezhnev's next two successors, transitional figures with deep roots in his tradition, did not last long. [Yuri Andropov](/wiki/Yuri_Andropov "Yuri Andropov") was 68 years old and [Konstantin Chernenko](/wiki/Konstantin_Chernenko "Konstantin Chernenko") 72 when they assumed power; both died in less than two years. In an attempt to avoid a third short-lived leader, in 1985, the Soviets turned to the next generation and selected [Mikhail Gorbachev](/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev "Mikhail Gorbachev"). He made significant changes in the economy and party leadership, called *[perestroika](/wiki/Perestroika "Perestroika")*. His policy of *[glasnost](/wiki/Glasnost "Glasnost")* freed public [access to information](/wiki/Freedom_of_information "Freedom of information") after decades of heavy government censorship. Gorbachev also moved to end the Cold War. In 1988, the USSR abandoned its [war in Afghanistan](/wiki/Soviet%E2%80%93Afghan_War "Soviet–Afghan War") and began to [withdraw its forces](/wiki/Soviet_withdrawal_from_Afghanistan "Soviet withdrawal from Afghanistan"). In the following year, [Gorbachev refused to interfere in the internal affairs of the Soviet satellite states](/wiki/Sinatra_Doctrine "Sinatra Doctrine"), which paved the way for the [Revolutions of 1989](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989"). In particular, the standstill of the Soviet Union at the [Pan-European Picnic](/wiki/Pan-European_Picnic "Pan-European Picnic") in August 1989 then set a peaceful chain reaction in motion, at the end of which the Eastern Bloc collapsed. With the tearing down of the [Berlin Wall](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Berlin_Wall "Fall of the Berlin Wall") and with East and West Germany pursuing re-unification, the [Iron Curtain](/wiki/Iron_Curtain "Iron Curtain") between [the West](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") and Soviet-occupied regions came down.[[336]](#cite_note-Andreas_Rödder_2009-351)[[337]](#cite_note-Thomas_Roser_2018-352)[[338]](#cite_note-Otmar_Lahodynsky_2014-353)

By 1989, the Soviet alliance system was on the brink of collapse, and, deprived of Soviet military support, the communist leaders of the Warsaw Pact states were losing power.[[329]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis2005235–236-344) Grassroots organizations, such as Poland's [Solidarity](/wiki/Solidarity_(Polish_trade_union) "Solidarity (Polish trade union)") movement, rapidly gained ground with strong popular bases.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/00_P%C3%A1neur%C3%B3pai_Piknik_eml%C3%A9khely.jpg/250px-00_P%C3%A1neur%C3%B3pai_Piknik_eml%C3%A9khely.jpg)](/wiki/File:00_P%C3%A1neur%C3%B3pai_Piknik_eml%C3%A9khely.jpg)

The [Pan-European Picnic](/wiki/Pan-European_Picnic "Pan-European Picnic") took place in August 1989 on the Hungarian-Austrian border.

The Pan-European Picnic in August 1989 in Hungary finally started a peaceful movement that the rulers in the Eastern Bloc could not stop. It was the largest movement of refugees from East Germany since the Berlin Wall was built in 1961 and ultimately brought about the fall of the Iron Curtain. The patrons of the picnic, [Otto von Habsburg](/wiki/Otto_von_Habsburg "Otto von Habsburg") and the Hungarian Minister of State [Imre Pozsgay](/wiki/Imre_Pozsgay "Imre Pozsgay"), saw the planned event as an opportunity to test Mikhail Gorbachev's reaction. The Austrian branch of the [Paneuropean Union](/wiki/Paneuropean_Union "Paneuropean Union"), which was then headed by Otto von Habsburg, distributed thousands of brochures inviting the GDR holidaymakers in Hungary to a picnic near the border at Sopron. But with the mass exodus at the Pan-European Picnic the subsequent hesitant behavior of the ruling Socialist Unity Party of East Germany and the non-interference of the Soviet Union broke the dams. Now tens of thousands of media-informed East Germans made their way to Hungary, which was no longer willing to keep its borders completely closed or to oblige its border troops to use armed force. On the one hand, this caused disagreement among the Eastern European states and, on the other hand, it was clear to the Eastern European population that the governments no longer had absolute power.[[336]](#cite_note-Andreas_Rödder_2009-351)[[337]](#cite_note-Thomas_Roser_2018-352)[[338]](#cite_note-Otmar_Lahodynsky_2014-353)[[339]](#cite_note-354)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R0518-182%2C_Erich_Honecker.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R0518-182%2C_Erich_Honecker.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-R0518-182,_Erich_Honecker.jpg)

East German leader [Erich Honecker](/wiki/Erich_Honecker "Erich Honecker") lost control in August 1989.

In 1989, the communist governments in Poland and Hungary became the first to negotiate the organization of competitive elections. In Czechoslovakia and East Germany, mass protests unseated entrenched communist leaders. The communist regimes in Bulgaria and Romania also crumbled, in the latter case as the result of a [violent uprising](/wiki/Romanian_Revolution "Romanian Revolution"). Attitudes had changed enough that US Secretary of State [James Baker](/wiki/James_Baker "James Baker") suggested that the American government would not be opposed to Soviet intervention in Romania, on behalf of the opposition, to prevent bloodshed.[[340]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGarthoff1994[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_August_2024]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (August_2024)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-355)

The tidal wave of change culminated with the [fall of the Berlin Wall](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Berlin_Wall "Fall of the Berlin Wall") in November 1989, which symbolized the collapse of European communist governments and graphically ended the Iron Curtain divide of Europe. The [1989 revolutionary wave](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989") swept across Central and Eastern Europe and peacefully overthrew all of the Soviet-style [Marxist–Leninist states](/wiki/Communist_state "Communist state"): East Germany, Poland, Hungary, Czechoslovakia and Bulgaria;[[341]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELefeberFitzmauriceVierdag1991221-356) Romania was the only Eastern-bloc country to topple its communist regime violently and execute its head of state.[[342]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis2005247-357)

### Soviet dissolution

Main article: [Dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union")

Further information: [History of the Soviet Union (1982–1991)](/wiki/History_of_the_Soviet_Union_(1982%E2%80%931991) "History of the Soviet Union (1982–1991)"), [The Barricades](/wiki/The_Barricades "The Barricades"), [1991 Soviet coup d'état attempt](/wiki/1991_Soviet_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_attempt "1991 Soviet coup d'état attempt"), [Commonwealth of Independent States](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Independent_States "Commonwealth of Independent States"), [Economy of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Soviet_Union "Economy of the Soviet Union"), and [Baltic Way](/wiki/Baltic_Way "Baltic Way")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Baltsk%C3%BD%C5%98et%C4%9Bz.jpg/250px-Baltsk%C3%BD%C5%98et%C4%9Bz.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baltsk%C3%BD%C5%98et%C4%9Bz.jpg)

The human chain in [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania") during the [Baltic Way](/wiki/Baltic_Way "Baltic Way"), 23 August 1989

At the same time, the Soviet republics started legal moves towards potentially declaring [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") over their territories, citing the freedom to secede in Article 72 of the USSR constitution.[[343]](#cite_note-358) On 7 April 1990, a law was passed allowing a republic to secede if more than two-thirds of its residents voted for it in a referendum.[[344]](#cite_note-359) Many held their first free elections in the Soviet era for their own national legislatures in 1990. Many of these legislatures proceeded to produce legislation contradicting the Union laws in what was known as the '[War of Laws](/wiki/War_of_Laws "War of Laws")'. In 1989, the [Russian SFSR](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") convened a newly elected Congress of People's Deputies. [Boris Yeltsin](/wiki/Boris_Yeltsin "Boris Yeltsin") was elected its chairman. On 12 June 1990, the Congress [declared Russia's sovereignty over its territory](/wiki/Declaration_of_State_Sovereignty_of_the_Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Declaration of State Sovereignty of the Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") and proceeded to pass laws that attempted to supersede some of the Soviet laws. After a landslide victory of [Sąjūdis](/wiki/S%C4%85j%C5%ABdis "Sąjūdis") in Lithuania, that country declared its independence restored on 11 March 1990, citing the illegality of the [Soviet occupation of the Baltic states](/wiki/Occupation_of_the_Baltic_states "Occupation of the Baltic states"). Soviet forces attempted to halt the secession by crushing popular demonstrations in Lithuania ([Bloody Sunday](/wiki/January_Events "January Events")) and Latvia ([The Barricades](/wiki/The_Barricades "The Barricades")), as a result, numerous civilians were killed or wounded. However, these actions only bolstered international support for the secessionists.[[345]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMälksoo2022[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_August_2024]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (August_2024)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-360)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/August_1991_coup_-_awaiting_the_counterattack_outside_the_White_House_Moscow_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-August_1991_coup_-_awaiting_the_counterattack_outside_the_White_House_Moscow_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:August_1991_coup_-_awaiting_the_counterattack_outside_the_White_House_Moscow_-_panoramio.jpg)

[August Coup](/wiki/1991_Soviet_coup_d%27%C3%A9tat_attempt "1991 Soviet coup d'état attempt") in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), 1991

A [referendum for the preservation of the USSR](/wiki/1991_Soviet_Union_referendum "1991 Soviet Union referendum") was held on 17 March 1991 in nine republics (the remainder having boycotted the vote), with the majority of the population in those republics voting for preservation of the Union in the form of a new federation. The referendum gave Gorbachev a minor boost. In the summer of 1991, the [New Union Treaty](/wiki/New_Union_Treaty "New Union Treaty"), which would have turned the country into a much looser Union, was agreed upon by eight republics. The signing of the treaty, however, was interrupted by the [August Coup](/wiki/1991_Soviet_coup_attempt "1991 Soviet coup attempt")—an attempted coup d'état by hardline members of the government and the KGB who sought to reverse Gorbachev's reforms and reassert the central government's control over the republics. After the coup collapsed, Russian president Yeltsin was seen as a hero for his decisive actions, while Gorbachev's power was effectively ended. The balance of power tipped significantly towards the republics. In August 1991, Latvia and Estonia immediately declared the restoration of their full independence (following Lithuania's 1990 example). Gorbachev resigned as general secretary in late August, and soon afterwards, the party's activities were indefinitely suspended—effectively ending its rule. By the fall, Gorbachev could no longer influence events outside Moscow, and he was being challenged even there by Yeltsin, who had been elected [President of Russia](/wiki/President_of_Russia "President of Russia") in July 1991.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Image0_ST.jpg/250px-Image0_ST.jpg)](/wiki/File:Image0_ST.jpg)

[T-80](/wiki/T-80 "T-80") tank on [Red Square](/wiki/Red_Square "Red Square") during the [August Coup](/wiki/1991_Soviet_coup_attempt "1991 Soviet coup attempt")

Later in August, Gorbachev resigned as [general secretary of the Communist party](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "General Secretary of the Communist Party of the Soviet Union"), and [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Soviet_Federative_Socialist_Republic "Russian Soviet Federative Socialist Republic") President Boris Yeltsin ordered the seizure of Soviet property. Gorbachev clung to power as the President of the Soviet Union until 25 December 1991, when the [USSR dissolved](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union").[[346]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGreene2015205–206-361) [Fifteen states](/wiki/Post-Soviet_states "Post-Soviet states") emerged from the Soviet Union, with by far the largest and most populous one (which also was the founder of the Soviet state with the [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") in Petrograd), the [Russian Federation](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), taking full responsibility for all the rights and obligations of the USSR under the Charter of the United Nations, including the financial obligations. As such, Russia assumed the Soviet Union's [UN membership and permanent membership on the Security Council](/wiki/Russia_and_the_United_Nations "Russia and the United Nations"), nuclear stockpile and the control over the armed forces.[[10]](#cite_note-web.archive.org-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/CCCP_1991_%284377719733%29.jpg/250px-CCCP_1991_%284377719733%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:CCCP_1991_(4377719733).jpg)

The first [Russian McDonald's](/wiki/McDonald%27s_in_Russia "McDonald's in Russia") on [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow")'s [Pushkin Square](/wiki/Pushkinskaya_Square "Pushkinskaya Square"), pictured in 1991

In his [1992 State of the Union Address](/wiki/1992_State_of_the_Union_Address "1992 State of the Union Address"), US President George H. W. Bush expressed his emotions: "The biggest thing that has happened in the world in my life, in our lives, is this: By the grace of God, America won the Cold War."[[347]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAmbroseBrinkley2011xvi-362) Bush and Yeltsin met in February 1992, declaring a new era of "friendship and partnership".[[348]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHanhimäkiSoutouGermond2010501-363) In January 1993, Bush and Yeltsin agreed to [START II](/wiki/START_II "START II"), which provided for further nuclear arms reductions on top of the original START treaty.[[349]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEvan_Dijk2013860–861-364)

## Aftermath

Main articles: [Effects of the Cold War](/wiki/Effects_of_the_Cold_War "Effects of the Cold War"), [International relations since 1989](/wiki/International_relations_since_1989 "International relations since 1989"), [Post-Soviet states](/wiki/Post-Soviet_states "Post-Soviet states"), [Post-Soviet conflicts](/wiki/Post-Soviet_conflicts "Post-Soviet conflicts"), [Yugoslav Wars](/wiki/Yugoslav_Wars "Yugoslav Wars"), [Second Cold War](/wiki/Second_Cold_War "Second Cold War"), and [East–West dichotomy](/wiki/East%E2%80%93West_dichotomy "East–West dichotomy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Changes_in_borders_post_cold_war.png/330px-Changes_in_borders_post_cold_war.png)](/wiki/File:Changes_in_borders_post_cold_war.png)

Changes in national boundaries after the end of the Cold War

In summing up the international ramifications of these events, [Vladislav Zubok](/wiki/Vladislav_Zubok "Vladislav Zubok") stated: 'The collapse of the [Soviet empire](/wiki/Soviet_empire "Soviet empire") was an event of epochal geopolitical, military, ideological, and economic significance.'[[350]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZubok2009ix-365) After the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union"), Russia drastically cut [military spending](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures "List of countries by military expenditures"), and restructuring the economy left millions unemployed.[[351]](#cite_note-366) According to Western analysis, the neoliberal reforms in Russia culminated in a [recession](/wiki/Recession "Recession") in the early 1990s more severe than the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") as experienced by the United States and Germany.[[352]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENolan199517–18-367) Western analysts suggest that in the 25 years following the end of the Cold War, only five or six of the post-communist states are on a path to joining the rich and capitalist world while most are falling behind, some to such an extent that it will take several decades to catch up to where they were before the collapse of communism.[[353]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGhodsee201763-368)[[354]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMilanović2015135–138-369)

### Decommunization

[Stephen Holmes](/wiki/Stephen_Holmes_(political_scientist) "Stephen Holmes (political scientist)") of the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago") argued in 1996 that [decommunization](/wiki/Decommunization "Decommunization"), after a brief active period, quickly ended in near-universal failure. After the introduction of [lustration](/wiki/Lustration "Lustration"), demand for scapegoats has become relatively low, and former communists have been elected for high governmental and other administrative positions. Holmes notes that the only real exception was former [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany"), where thousands of former [Stasi](/wiki/Stasi "Stasi") informers have been fired from public positions.[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMandelbaum1996[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_August_2024]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (August_2024)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-370)

Holmes suggests the following reasons for the failure of decommunization:[[355]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMandelbaum1996[[Category:Wikipedia_articles_needing_page_number_citations_from_August_2024]]<sup_class="noprint_Inline-Template_"_style="white-space:nowrap;">[<i>[[Wikipedia:Citing_sources|<span_title="This_citation_requires_a_reference_to_the_specific_page_or_range_of_pages_in_which_the_material_appears. (August_2024)">page needed</span>]]</i>]</sup>-370)

* After 45–70 years of communist rule, nearly every family has members associated with the state. After the initial desire "to root out the reds" came a realization that massive punishment is wrong and finding only some guilty is hardly justice.
* The urgency of the current economic problems of postcommunism makes the crimes of the communist past "old news" for many citizens.
* Decommunization is believed to be a power game of elites.
* The difficulty of dislodging the social elite makes it require a [totalitarian state](/wiki/Totalitarian_state "Totalitarian state") to disenfranchise the "[enemies of the people](/wiki/Enemies_of_the_people "Enemies of the people")" quickly and efficiently and a desire for normalcy overcomes the desire for punitive justice.
* Very few people have a perfectly clean slate and so are available to fill the positions that require significant expertise.

Compared with the decommunization efforts of the other former constituents of the [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc") and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), decommunization in Russia has been restricted to half-measures, if conducted at all.[[356]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERyavec200313-371) Notable anti-communist measures in the Russian Federation include the banning of the [Communist Party of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Soviet_Union "Communist Party of the Soviet Union") (and the creation of the [Communist Party of the Russian Federation](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Russian_Federation "Communist Party of the Russian Federation")) as well as changing the names of some Russian cities back to what they were before the 1917 [October Revolution](/wiki/October_Revolution "October Revolution") (Leningrad to [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg"), Sverdlovsk to [Yekaterinburg](/wiki/Yekaterinburg "Yekaterinburg") and Gorky to [Nizhny Novgorod](/wiki/Nizhny_Novgorod "Nizhny Novgorod")),[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShevchenko2015-372) though others were maintained, with [Ulyanovsk](/wiki/Ulyanovsk "Ulyanovsk") (former Simbirsk), [Tolyatti](/wiki/Tolyatti "Tolyatti") (former Stavropol) and [Kirov](/wiki/Kirov,_Kirov_Oblast "Kirov, Kirov Oblast") (former Vyatka) being examples. Even though Leningrad and Sverdlovsk were renamed, regions that were named after them are still officially called Leningrad and Sverdlovsk oblasts.[[358]](#cite_note-373)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/The_Spasskaya_Tower%2C_photographed_in_1883%2C_published_1884_%28Saviour_Tower_-_%D0%A1%D0%BF%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%B1%D0%B0%D1%88%D0%BD%D1%8F%29%2C_Moscow_Kremlin%2C_Red_Square.jpg/250px-The_Spasskaya_Tower%2C_photographed_in_1883%2C_published_1884_%28Saviour_Tower_-_%D0%A1%D0%BF%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%B1%D0%B0%D1%88%D0%BD%D1%8F%29%2C_Moscow_Kremlin%2C_Red_Square.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Spasskaya_Tower,_photographed_in_1883,_published_1884_(Saviour_Tower_-_%D0%A1%D0%BF%D0%B0%D1%81%D1%81%D0%BA%D0%B0%D1%8F_%D0%B1%D0%B0%D1%88%D0%BD%D1%8F),_Moscow_Kremlin,_Red_Square.jpg)

The [Spasskaya Tower](/wiki/Spasskaya_Tower "Spasskaya Tower") had kept its red star and did not restore the two-headed eagle present before communist takeover.

[Nostalgia for the Soviet Union](/wiki/Nostalgia_for_the_Soviet_Union "Nostalgia for the Soviet Union") is gradually on the rise in Russia.[[359]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERosenberg2016-374) Communist symbols continue to form an important part of the rhetoric used in [state-controlled media](/wiki/Media_of_Russia "Media of Russia"), as banning on them in other countries is seen by the [Russian foreign ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Foreign_Affairs_(Russia) "Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Russia)") as "sacrilege" and "a perverse idea of good and evil".[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShevchenko2015-372) The process of [decommunization in Ukraine](/wiki/Decommunization_in_Ukraine "Decommunization in Ukraine"), a neighbouring [post-Soviet state](/wiki/Post-Soviet_state "Post-Soviet state"), was met with fierce criticism by Russia.[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShevchenko2015-372) The [State Anthem of the Russian Federation](/wiki/State_Anthem_of_the_Russian_Federation "State Anthem of the Russian Federation"), adopted in 2000 (the same year [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") began his first term as president of Russia), uses the exact same music as the [State Anthem of the Soviet Union](/wiki/State_Anthem_of_the_Soviet_Union "State Anthem of the Soviet Union"), but with new lyrics written by [Sergey Mikhalkov](/wiki/Sergey_Mikhalkov "Sergey Mikhalkov").[[360]](#cite_note-375)[[361]](#cite_note-376)

Conversely, decommunization in Ukraine started during and after the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") in 1991.[[362]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKhotin2009-377) With the success of the [Revolution of Dignity](/wiki/Revolution_of_Dignity "Revolution of Dignity") in 2014, the [Ukrainian government](/wiki/Ukrainian_government "Ukrainian government") approved [laws](/wiki/Ukrainian_decommunization_laws "Ukrainian decommunization laws") that outlawed [communist symbols](/wiki/Communist_symbol "Communist symbol").[[363]](#cite_note-ectniiU-378) In July 2015, President of Ukraine [Petro Poroshenko](/wiki/Petro_Poroshenko "Petro Poroshenko") signed a set of laws that started a six-month period for the removal of communist monuments (excluding [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") monuments) and renaming of public places named after communist-related themes.[[357]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShevchenko2015-372)[[364]](#cite_note-379)[[365]](#cite_note-380) At the time, this meant that 22 cities and 44 villages were set to get new names.[[366]](#cite_note-381) In 2016, 51,493 streets and 987 cities and villages were renamed, and 1,320 [Lenin monuments](/wiki/Lenin_monuments "Lenin monuments") and 1,069 monuments to other communist figures removed.[[367]](#cite_note-rdiU16-382) Violation of the law carries a penalty of a potential media ban and prison sentences of up to five years.[[368]](#cite_note-dwdc9415-383)[[369]](#cite_note-oscedc18515-384) The [Ministry of the Interior](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Interior_(Ukraine) "Ministry of the Interior (Ukraine)") stripped the [Communist Party of Ukraine](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Ukraine "Communist Party of Ukraine"), the [Communist Party of Ukraine (renewed)](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Ukraine_(renewed) "Communist Party of Ukraine (renewed)"), and the [Communist Party of Workers and Peasants](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Workers_and_Peasants "Communist Party of Workers and Peasants") of their right to participate in elections and stated it was continuing the court actions that started in July 2014 to end the registration of [communist parties in Ukraine](/wiki/Category:Communist_parties_in_Ukraine "Category:Communist parties in Ukraine").[[370]](#cite_note-Banukcom24715-385) By 16 December 2015, these three parties had been banned in Ukraine; the Communist Party of Ukraine appealed the ban to the [European Court of Human Rights](/wiki/European_Court_of_Human_Rights "European Court of Human Rights").[[371]](#cite_note-386)[[372]](#cite_note-387)[[373]](#cite_note-388)

### Collapse of Yugoslavia and Balkan conflicts

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Nato_flyer.jpg/250px-Nato_flyer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nato_flyer.jpg)

NATO [psyop](/wiki/Psyop "Psyop") flyer during the [Kosovo War](/wiki/Kosovo_War "Kosovo War") 1999.[[374]](#cite_note-389)

The Cold War had provided external stabilizing pressures. Both the United States and the Soviet Union had a vested interest in Yugoslavia's stability, ensuring it remained a buffer state in the east–west divide. This resulted in financial and political support for its regime. When the Cold War ended, this external support evaporated, leaving Yugoslavia more vulnerable to internal divisions.[[375]](#cite_note-390)[[376]](#cite_note-391)

As Yugoslavia fragmented, [the wars](/wiki/Yugoslav_Wars "Yugoslav Wars") began after [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") and [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") declared independence in 1991. [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"), under [Slobodan Milošević](/wiki/Slobodan_Milo%C5%A1evi%C4%87 "Slobodan Milošević"), opposed these moves.[[377]](#cite_note-392) [The Bosnian War](/wiki/Bosnian_War "Bosnian War") (1992–1995) was the most brutal of the Yugoslav Wars, characterized by ethnic cleansing and genocide. International organizations, including the United Nations, struggled to manage the violence. NATO eventually intervened with airstrikes in Bosnia (1995) as part of [Operation Deliberate Force](/wiki/Operation_Deliberate_Force "Operation Deliberate Force") and later in Kosovo (1999) as part of [Operation Allied Force](/wiki/Operation_allied_force "Operation allied force"). These interventions marked the transition of NATO as a deterrent to the Soviet Union, to also functioning at the time as an active peacekeeping and conflict-resolution force.[[378]](#cite_note-393)

## Influence

The post-Cold War world is considered to be [unipolar](/wiki/Polarity_(international_relations)#Unipolarity "Polarity (international relations)"), with the United States the sole remaining [superpower](/wiki/Superpower "Superpower").[[379]](#cite_note-394)[[380]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlum200687-395) The Cold War defined the political role of the United States after World War II—by 1989 the United States had military alliances with 50 countries, with 526,000 troops stationed abroad,[[381]](#cite_note-PBS_2004-396) with 326,000 in Europe (two-thirds of which were in [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany"))[[382]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuke1989175-397) and 130,000 in Asia (mainly [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea")).[[381]](#cite_note-PBS_2004-396) The Cold War also marked the zenith of peacetime [military–industrial complexes](/wiki/Military%E2%80%93industrial_complex "Military–industrial complex") and large-scale [military funding of science](/wiki/History_of_military_technology "History of military technology").[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalhoun2002-398)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Enlargement_of_the_European_Union_77.gif/250px-Enlargement_of_the_European_Union_77.gif)](/wiki/File:Enlargement_of_the_European_Union_77.gif)

Since the end of the Cold War, the [EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") has [expanded eastwards](/wiki/Enlargement_of_the_European_Union "Enlargement of the European Union") into the former Warsaw Pact and parts of the former Soviet Union.

Cumulative US military expenditures throughout the entire Cold War amounted to an estimated $8 trillion. Nearly 100,000 Americans died in the [Korean](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") and [Vietnam Wars](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War").[[384]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELaFeber20021-399) Although Soviet casualties are difficult to estimate, as a share of gross national product the financial cost for the Soviet Union was much higher than that incurred by the United States.[[385]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis2005213-400)

Millions died in the superpowers' [proxy wars](/wiki/Proxy_war "Proxy war") around the globe, most notably in eastern Asia.[[386]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGaddis2005266-401)[[P]](#cite_note-402) Most of the proxy wars and subsidies for local conflicts ended along with the Cold War; interstate wars, ethnic wars, revolutionary wars, as well as refugee and displaced persons crises have declined sharply in the post-Cold War years.[[387]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarshallGurr2006-403)

The aftermath of the Cold War is, however, not considered to be concluded. Many of the economic and social tensions that were exploited to fuel Cold War competition in parts of the Third World remain acute. The breakdown of state control in a number of areas formerly ruled by communist governments produced new civil and ethnic conflicts, particularly in the former [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia"). In Central and Eastern Europe, the end of the Cold War has ushered in an era of [economic growth](/wiki/Economic_growth "Economic growth") and an increase in the number of [liberal democracies](/wiki/Liberal_democracy "Liberal democracy"), while in other parts of the world, such as Afghanistan, independence was accompanied by [state failure](/wiki/Failed_state "Failed state").[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalliday20012e-273) It has been posited by several scholars that the [dissolution of the Soviet Union](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Soviet_Union "Dissolution of the Soviet Union") and the end of [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") as a global force in the post-Cold War era allowed [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberal "Neoliberal") [capitalism](/wiki/Capitalism "Capitalism") to become the dominant global system, which has resulted in rising [economic inequality](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality").[[388]](#cite_note-404)[[389]](#cite_note-405)[[390]](#cite_note-406)[[391]](#cite_note-407)

## In popular culture

See also: [Culture during the Cold War](/wiki/Culture_during_the_Cold_War "Culture during the Cold War")

The Cold War endures as a popular topic reflected in entertainment media, and continuing to the present with Cold War-themed feature films, novels, television and web series, and other media.

## Historiography

Main article: [Historiography of the Cold War](/wiki/Historiography_of_the_Cold_War "Historiography of the Cold War")

Interpreting the course and origins of the conflict has been a source of heated controversy among historians, political scientists, and journalists.[[392]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENashel1999-408) In particular, historians have sharply disagreed as to who was responsible for the breakdown of Soviet–US relations after the Second World War; and whether the conflict between the two superpowers was inevitable or could have been avoided.[[393]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAmbroseBrinkley2011789–799-409) Historians have also disagreed on what exactly the Cold War was, what the sources of the conflict were, and how to disentangle patterns of action and reaction between the two sides.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalliday20012e-273)

Although explanations of the origins of the conflict in academic discussions are complex and diverse, several general schools of thought on the subject can be identified. Historians commonly speak of three different approaches to the study of the Cold War: "orthodox" accounts, "revisionism", and "post-revisionism".[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalhoun2002-398)

"Orthodox" accounts place responsibility for the Cold War on the Soviet Union and its expansion further into Europe.[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalhoun2002-398) "Revisionist" writers place more responsibility for the breakdown of post-war peace on the United States, citing a range of US efforts to isolate and confront the Soviet Union well before the end of World War II.[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalhoun2002-398) "Post-revisionists" see the events of the Cold War as more nuanced and attempt to be more balanced in determining what occurred during the Cold War.[[383]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalhoun2002-398) Much of the historiography on the Cold War weaves together two or even all three of these broad categories.[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEByrd2003-84)
